bytes.topic: Testing Strategy — unit vs integration vs E2E pyramid, what to test at each level
bytes.title: vitest Testing Pyramid — What to Test at Every Layer
bytes.body:
Vitest sits at the unit and integration layers of the pyramid; Playwright owns E2E. Misplacing tests is the most expensive mistake in a frontend test suite.

Unit tests (the wide base): pure functions, hooks in isolation via `renderHook`, and Zod schemas. Use `vi.fn()` to stub dependencies. Keep them sub-10ms — vitest's thread-pool makes this trivial.

Integration tests (the middle): render a real component tree with `@testing-library/react`, but mock the network with `vi.mock('~/lib/api')` or intercept via `msw` handlers loaded in `setupFiles`. These validate that components wire props, state, and callbacks correctly without a browser.

E2E tests (the narrow tip): let Playwright own this. Only cover critical user journeys — auth flow, checkout, form submission. Never duplicate in vitest what Playwright already asserts.

The forcing function: if a vitest test requires a browser API (`window.matchMedia`, `IntersectionObserver`), either mock it in `setupFiles` or reconsider whether it belongs in Playwright. Leaking browser-specific concerns into vitest slows the suite and signals a layer violation.

bytes.topic: Test Behaviour Not Implementation — Testing Library philosophy, query by role not by class
bytes.title: vitest + Testing Library — Query by Role, Not by Class
bytes.body:
The Testing Library philosophy baked into @testing-library/react pairs naturally with vitest: test what the user sees, not how your component is built internally. Querying by CSS class or test ID ties your tests to implementation details — rename a class and your suite breaks for no user-facing reason.

Prefer `getByRole` over `getByTestId` or `querySelector`. Roles map to ARIA semantics: a `<button>` is `button`, a `<nav>` is `navigation`, an `<input type="checkbox">` is `checkbox`. When multiple elements share a role, narrow with `name` (the accessible label). Use `getByLabelText` for form fields — it validates your `<label>` wiring as a side-effect.

In vitest, this philosophy compounds with `expect` from `@testing-library/jest-dom`: matchers like `toBeVisible()`, `toBeDisabled()`, and `toHaveValue()` assert on user-observable state, not DOM structure. If a refactor doesn't change user behaviour, zero tests should break. That's the contract — and vitest's fast HMR-style watch mode makes enforcing it frictionless.

bytes.topic: Component Testing — rendering, user interaction, asserting on visible output
bytes.title: Vitest Component Testing — Render, Interact, Assert with @testing-library/react
bytes.body:
Vitest integrates seamlessly with @testing-library/react via `jsdom` or `happy-dom` as the test environment. Set `environment: 'jsdom'` in `vitest.config.ts` and you're ready to mount components and drive them like a real user.

`render()` mounts your component into a virtual DOM. Query with `screen.getByRole`, `screen.getByText`, or `screen.findByText` for async updates — all return real DOM nodes, never implementation internals. Fire events with `userEvent` (preferred over `fireEvent`) since it simulates the full browser event chain including focus, keyboard, and pointer events.

Assertions live in `@testing-library/jest-dom`, which Vitest picks up automatically when you add `setupFiles: ['@testing-library/jest-dom/vitest']`. Matchers like `toBeVisible()`, `toHaveValue()`, and `toBeDisabled()` assert on what the user actually sees, not component state.

Use `vi.fn()` to stub callbacks passed as props, then assert call count and arguments with `expect(fn).toHaveBeenCalledWith(...)`. For async interactions — data fetching, transitions — await `screen.findBy*` instead of wrapping in `waitFor`; it retries automatically until the assertion passes or times out.

bytes.topic: Unit Testing — pure functions, custom hooks with renderHook
bytes.title: Vitest — Unit Testing Pure Functions and Custom Hooks with renderHook
bytes.body:
Pure functions are the easiest win in Vitest: import, call, assert. No setup, no mocks, instant feedback via `vitest --watch`. Where it gets interesting is custom hooks. Vitest pairs with `@testing-library/react` to expose `renderHook`, which mounts a hook in an isolated wrapper so you can assert against its return values without a real component.

Use `act` from `@testing-library/react` (not React directly) to flush state updates — Vitest's fake timers integrate cleanly with it via `vi.useFakeTimers()`. For hooks that depend on context, pass a `wrapper` option to `renderHook` with your provider tree.

For pure functions, lean on `it.each` to table-drive edge cases — Vitest's implementation is fully typed and supports template literal test names. Avoid `describe` nesting deeper than two levels; flat test files with clear `it` descriptions read faster in Vitest's terminal reporter. Snapshot testing pure transformations with `toMatchInlineSnapshot` keeps expected output co-located with the assertion, eliminating snapshot file sprawl.

bytes.topic: Integration Testing — testing multiple components together, mocking at the right boundary
bytes.title: vitest Integration Testing — Mock at the Network Boundary, Not the Component Boundary
bytes.body: Integration tests in vitest shine when you render a component tree together and only mock what crosses a real boundary — network calls, browser APIs, timers. The mistake most teams make is mocking child components, which guts the test's value. If `<UserProfile>` renders `<Avatar>` and `<ActivityFeed>`, test them as a unit. Use `msw` with vitest's `setupFiles` to intercept HTTP at the fetch level instead. Configure `server.use(http.get('/api/user/:id', ...))` in a `beforeEach`, then render the parent with `@testing-library/react`. Assertions target real DOM output — `getByRole`, `findByText` — not internal state. Vitest's `vi.mock` is still the right tool for non-HTTP boundaries: browser storage, `crypto`, `IntersectionObserver`. Use `vi.stubGlobal('IntersectionObserver', mockImpl)` rather than polluting the module graph. The rule: mock the protocol, not the component. Your integration test should survive a full component refactor without changing a single assertion.

bytes.topic: E2E Testing — full user flows, critical paths only (auth, onboarding, core feature)
bytes.title: vitest Browser Mode — E2E Critical Paths Without JSDOM Lies
bytes.body:
Vitest's browser mode (`@vitest/browser`) runs tests inside a real Chromium, Firefox, or WebKit instance via a Playwright provider — no JSDOM approximations of clicks, focus, or paint. Configure it in `vitest.config.ts` with `browser: { enabled: true, provider: 'playwright', name: 'chromium' }` and scope a dedicated workspace project to auth, onboarding, and your core feature only.

Inside these tests, import `page` and `userEvent` from `@vitest/browser/context` instead of `@testing-library/user-event`. These APIs drive real browser events and await actual paint cycles. Swap `expect()` for `expect.element()` on locators — it retries until the element satisfies the matcher or times out, eliminating manual `waitFor` calls.

Structure: one file per critical path, no branching scenarios. Run them with `vitest run --project browser` in CI, isolated from your unit workspace so a JSDOM failure never blocks browser tests and vice versa. If a critical-path test is flaky, treat it as a component bug — never add `retry` to paper over it. Reserve E2E coverage for flows where a broken path means lost users: login, signup, and the one action that defines your product.

bytes.topic: Mocking — MSW for API mocking, when to mock vs use real dependencies
bytes.title: vitest + MSW — When to Mock the Network and When to Stop
bytes.body:
MSW intercepts at the network level, not the module level — your fetch/axios code runs unmodified, only the response changes. In vitest, use `msw/node` with `setupServer`, not the browser service worker. Wire it up once in `setupFilesAfterFramework` or per-suite with `beforeAll`/`afterAll`, and reset handlers with `server.resetHandlers()` in `afterEach` to prevent handler bleed between tests.

When to mock: external APIs you don't own, services with rate limits, flaky third-party endpoints, or anything that would make `vitest --run` non-deterministic. When NOT to mock: your own backend endpoints during integration tests — use a real test DB and actual HTTP calls instead. Mocking your own API just means you're testing your mock, not your contract.

Avoid `vi.mock('axios')` or `vi.mock('node-fetch')` for HTTP — MSW is strictly better because it catches all transports, validates request shape, and surfaces unexpected outbound calls as test failures when you enable `server.listen({ onUnhandledRequest: 'error' })`.

bytes.topic: Async Testing — waitFor, findBy queries, handling promises and fake timers
bytes.title: vitest Async Testing — waitFor, findBy, and Fake Timers Done Right
bytes.body:
vitest ships with full support for async testing patterns that pair directly with Testing Library. When asserting on elements that appear after an async operation, prefer `findBy*` queries over `getBy*` — they return a Promise and retry until the element appears or the timeout expires. For more complex async state changes, `waitFor` lets you wrap any assertion and poll until it passes, respecting vitest's default 1000ms timeout.

For code using `setTimeout` or `setInterval`, reach for `vi.useFakeTimers()`. After setting up fakes, call `vi.runAllTimers()` or `vi.advanceTimersByTime(ms)` to fast-forward without real delays. Always call `vi.useRealTimers()` in `afterEach` to prevent timer leakage between tests.

Avoid mixing `vi.useFakeTimers()` with `waitFor` — fake timers stall the polling loop inside `waitFor`. Instead, advance timers manually, then assert directly. For promise-based flows, `await vi.runAllTimersAsync()` (vitest 1.x+) drains the microtask queue alongside fake timers cleanly.

bytes.topic: Snapshot Testing — when it helps vs when it becomes noise
bytes.title: Vitest Snapshot Testing — When It Helps vs When It Becomes Noise
bytes.body:
Snapshot tests in Vitest shine for stable, serialized output — a utility that formats error objects, a pure transformer, or a small presentational component's rendered HTML. When the shape rarely changes, `toMatchSnapshot()` acts as a cheap regression net: it fails fast on unintended structural drift without hand-coding every assertion.

The noise starts when snapshots grow large, get auto-updated reflexively with `vitest -u`, or wrap components that change every sprint. A 300-line snapshot of a data table is meaningless in review — nobody reads it, reviewers approve blindly, and the test provides false confidence. Prefer `toMatchInlineSnapshot()` for small, intentional snapshots readable inline; bloated inline snapshots are immediately painful, which forces discipline.

Practical rule: if you're running `vitest --update-snapshots` more than once a week on the same file, replace those tests with targeted assertions like `expect(node.textContent).toBe(...)`. Snapshots should document stable contracts, not paper over missing assertions. The `__snapshots__` directory growing unchecked is a code smell, not a test suite.

bytes.topic: Visual Regression — Chromatic with Storybook, screenshot diffing in CI
bytes.title: vitest + Storybook: Visual Regression with Chromatic and the storybookTest Plugin
bytes.body:
Storybook 8's `storybookTest` vitest plugin transforms your `.stories.tsx` files into native vitest test suites — no separate test runner needed. Configure a `storybook` project in `vitest.config.ts` with `browser.provider: 'playwright'` and every story executes headlessly via Chromium. Vitest validates interactions through `play()` functions using `@storybook/test`'s `userEvent` and `expect` wrappers, which delegate to vitest's own assertion engine under the hood.

For visual regression, wire Chromatic into CI after your vitest run. Chromatic snapshots every story on each push, diffs pixel changes across branches, and blocks PRs on unreviewed UI changes. Because stories already execute as vitest tests, a single CI step — `vitest run --project=storybook` — catches both interaction regressions and surfaces the component states Chromatic will diff.

Critical gotcha: set `browser.screenshotFailures: false` inside your storybook project config. Without it, vitest generates its own failure screenshots that conflict with Chromatic's baseline management, producing false positives in your diff queue.

bytes.topic: Accessibility Testing — axe-core integration, automated a11y checks in tests
bytes.title: vitest + axe-core — Automated Accessibility Checks Without a Browser
bytes.body: vitest runs in a jsdom environment, which means you can catch accessibility violations at unit-test speed using `@axe-core/react` or the lower-level `axe-core` directly via `jest-axe` (fully compatible with vitest). After rendering a component with `@testing-library/react`, pass the container to `axe()` and assert with `toHaveNoViolations()`. The matcher is added once in your `setupFiles` entry. This catches missing `aria-label` attributes, invalid roles, poor color contrast metadata, and heading hierarchy errors before they reach a browser. Scope scans to a specific subtree by passing `{ include: [['.modal']] }` as the axe options argument — this cuts noise when testing isolated components inside a larger DOM. Pair with `vitest`'s `test.each` to run the same a11y assertion across multiple prop variants (e.g., different button states: disabled, loading, error). Unlike Playwright a11y audits, this runs in-process with zero network overhead, making it practical to add to every component test file.

bytes.topic: Test Data Management — factories, fixtures, seeding test state
bytes.title: vitest Test Data Management — Factories, Fixtures, and test.extend()
bytes.body:
Hard-coded objects scattered across test files break silently when shapes change. The fix: factory functions paired with vitest's first-class fixture system.

A factory is a plain function that returns a valid object with sane defaults, accepting partial overrides. TypeScript's `Partial<T>` keeps it type-safe without forcing callers to specify every field.

For stateful setup — DB rows, auth sessions, pre-populated stores — use `test.extend()`. It lets you declare typed fixtures that are constructed before each test and torn down after, with full async support. Each fixture receives already-resolved peer fixtures, so you can compose them: an `authedClient` fixture can depend on a `user` fixture. Vitest handles the dependency graph.

Avoid `beforeEach` for complex seeding; it bleeds state implicitly across every test in the suite. `test.extend()` makes dependencies explicit and co-located with the tests that need them.

For shared read-only data (enum-like constants, static API responses), use module-level `const` — no fixture overhead needed. Reserve fixtures for anything that mutates or needs isolation.

bytes.topic: CI Integration — parallelizing tests, caching, fail-fast strategies
bytes.title: Vitest CI Integration — Parallelizing, Caching, and Fail-Fast for Faster Pipelines
bytes.body:
Vitest's built-in worker pool makes parallelism easy, but CI environments need explicit tuning. Set `--reporter=verbose` for clear CI output and use `--reporter=junit` to emit JUnit XML for test result dashboards.

For parallelism, Vitest shards natively: `vitest run --shard=1/4` splits your suite across 4 CI jobs without external tooling. Each runner gets a deterministic subset based on file hashing — no coordination needed.

Cache the Vite transform cache (`node_modules/.vite`) and Vitest's snapshot directory between runs. On GitHub Actions, key the cache on `package-lock.json` hash plus `vitest.config.ts` hash — config changes bust the transform cache correctly.

Fail-fast is controlled via `--bail=N`: `--bail=1` stops the entire run after the first failure, freeing CI minutes early. Combine with `--no-coverage` in fail-fast scenarios — coverage instrumentation adds 30–50% overhead and is useless if the suite is already broken.

For monorepos, scope runs with `vitest run packages/ui` rather than root-level runs to avoid testing unaffected workspaces.
bytes.code_snippet:
# .github/workflows/test.yml (relevant steps)
- name: Cache Vite/Vitest artifacts
  uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: |
      node_modules/.vite
      **/__snapshots__
    key: vitest-${{ hashFiles('package-lock.json', 'vitest.config.ts') }}

- name: Run shard ${{ matrix.shard }}
  run: npx vitest run --shard=${{ matrix.shard }}/4 --bail=1 --reporter=junit --outputFile=results.xml

strategy:
  fail-fast: true
  matrix:
    shard: [1, 2, 3, 4]

bytes.topic: Vitest-specific — vite-native speed, compatible Jest API, in-source testing
bytes.title: Vitest — Vite-Native Speed, Jest Compatibility, and In-Source Testing
bytes.body:
Vitest runs inside Vite's dev server, sharing the same config, plugins, and module graph. That means zero cold-start overhead — your tests resolve the same aliases and transforms your app already uses, without a separate bundler pass.

The API is a deliberate Jest superset: `describe`, `it`, `expect`, `vi.fn()`, `vi.spyOn()`, and `vi.mock()` all behave as expected. Migration from Jest is usually a find-and-replace on imports — swap `jest` for `vi`.

The sharpest Vitest-exclusive feature is in-source testing. Add an `if (import.meta.vitest)` block directly inside your source file. Vitest strips it in production builds (Vite tree-shakes it), but executes it during test runs. This keeps unit tests physically co-located with the function they test — no separate spec file needed for pure utilities.

Pair in-source tests with `globals: true` in `vitest.config.ts` to drop the `import { it, expect } from 'vitest'` boilerplate inside those blocks. Use `pool: 'vmThreads'` for faster parallelism over the default `forks` pool on CPU-bound suites.

bytes.topic: Playwright-specific — multi-browser, trace viewer, network interception
bytes.title: vitest Browser Mode — Multi-Browser, Traces, and Real Network Interception with Playwright
bytes.body:
Vitest's browser mode (`@vitest/browser`) runs tests inside a real browser using Playwright as the provider — giving you access to Playwright's full API surface directly in your test files. Configure `instances` in `vitest.config.ts` to fan out a single test suite across Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit simultaneously, with results aggregated per-browser in the terminal.

Network interception works via the `page` fixture from `@vitest/browser/context`. Call `page.route()` before rendering your component — Playwright intercepts at the actual network layer, not via module mocking. This catches issues `vi.mock('fetch')` cannot: malformed response handling, redirect chains, and CORS edge cases.

Traces are captured with `providerOptions.launch.tracesDir` in your vitest config. When a test fails, open it with `npx playwright show-trace .vitest-traces/<name>.zip` — you get DOM snapshots, network waterfall, and console logs at every step. Pair with `--reporter=html` to correlate vitest's test output with the trace timeline.

bytes.title: Vitest Browser Mode — Component Testing and the cy.intercept Equivalent
bytes.body:
Vitest's browser mode (`@vitest/browser`) runs component tests in a real Chromium/Firefox/WebKit instance via Playwright or WebdriverIO, mirroring Cypress's component testing mode. But there's no `cy.intercept` equivalent built in — network mocking falls to MSW.

In jsdom mode (the default), `setupServer` from `msw/node` intercepts fetch at the Node layer. In true browser mode, you need `setupWorker` with a registered service worker, or the `vitest-msw` integration that handles worker setup automatically.

Critical difference from Cypress: `vi.mock` cannot intercept real HTTP calls in browser mode — it only stubs ES module imports. For any component that calls `fetch` or `axios`, MSW is the correct interception layer.

Handler scoping follows the same `beforeAll / afterEach / afterAll` pattern as Jest + MSW, so the mental model transfers cleanly. Override handlers per-test with `server.use(...)` to simulate error or loading states — the direct vitest equivalent of a `cy.intercept` override scoped inside an `it` block.

bytes.topic: Storybook — component documentation, interaction testing, story-driven development
bytes.title: vitest + Storybook — Story play() Functions as Native Browser Tests
bytes.body:
Storybook's `@storybook/addon-vitest` plugin turns every `.stories.tsx` file into a vitest test suite with zero extra boilerplate. Add `storybookTest()` to `vitest.config.ts`, point vitest at a Playwright browser provider, and each named story export becomes a discrete test case automatically.

The lever is the `play` function. Authored with `@storybook/test` — which re-exports `userEvent` and `expect` directly from vitest — play functions run inside a real Chromium tab during `vitest run`. Your `SubmitsForm` story's interaction sequence is now a browser-mode vitest test. Same assertion API, no duplication, CI-ready.

For jsdom-level tests, `composeStories()` from `@storybook/react` imports stories as fully-decorated components. Wrap them in `@testing-library/react`'s `render()` and assert with vitest's `expect` — all decorators, args, and context applied automatically.

The payoff: every UI state you document in a story is a regression test. Write the error-state story, add a `play` function — vitest catches the break in CI before review. Story-driven development stops being documentation theater and becomes your actual test coverage.

bytes.topic: Code Coverage — what it tells you and what it doesn't, avoiding coverage theater
bytes.title: Vitest Code Coverage — What It Tells You and What It Doesn't
bytes.body:
Running `vitest --coverage` with `@vitest/coverage-v8` gives you line, branch, function, and statement metrics — but 100% coverage is not a correctness guarantee. Coverage measures execution paths hit, not assertions made. A test that calls a function without `expect()` inflates your numbers while catching nothing.

The real trap is branch coverage theater. A ternary with both arms covered doesn't mean your logic is correct — it means both paths ran. Use `coverage.thresholds` in `vitest.config.ts` to enforce minimums, but pair it with meaningful assertions.

Vitest's `coverage.include` and `coverage.exclude` let you scope reports precisely — exclude generated files, route manifests, and index re-exports that add noise. Focus threshold enforcement on `src/lib` and `src/hooks` where logic lives.

The `all: true` option under `coverage` forces uncovered files into the report — critical for spotting dead code that tests never import. Without it, a file with zero tests simply disappears from your metrics, silently inflating your score.
